evidence_note: Summarized from official NHLBI insomnia materials. This file is a source-grounded raw outline for synthetic case generation, not a diagnostic guideline.

raw_outline.summary: Insomnia involves difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, waking too early, or having poor-quality sleep with daytime impairment. Chronic insomnia is considered when sleep difficulty occurs at least 3 nights per week for 3 months or longer.
core_features: Difficulty falling asleep is common in younger adults.
Waking frequently or being awake for much of the night is common and especially affects older adults.
Early-morning awakening and nonrestorative sleep can occur.
Poor sleep may lead to daytime sleepiness, poor focus, anxiety, depression, or irritability.
common_symptoms: Short sleep duration or being awake for much of the night
Early-morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Unrested feeling, daytime sleepiness, poor concentration, anxiety, depressed mood, or irritability
course_and_timing: Diagnosis considers frequency, duration, sleep habits, daytime impairment, risk factors, medical history, and family history.
A sleep diary for 1 to 2 weeks can help characterize patterns.
Sleep study, actigraphy, or blood tests may be used when another sleep disorder or medical condition is suspected.
risk_factors: Shift work, night work, travel across time zones, noise, light, or uncomfortable temperatures
Changing routines, caring for others at night, long daytime naps, too little daytime activity, caffeine, nicotine, alcohol, recreational drugs, or electronic-device use near bedtime
Stress related to school, work, relationships, money, bereavement, or worry about sleep

generation_severity_anchors.mild: Sleep difficulty on several nights with mild daytime fatigue or irritability, but routine function is mostly preserved.
generation_severity_anchors.moderate: Persistent insomnia with impaired concentration, mood, or role performance; clear maladaptive sleep habits or stress-related perpetuating factors.
generation_severity_anchors.severe: Chronic insomnia with major daytime impairment, marked anxiety/depression, unsafe sleepiness, suspected sleep apnea/narcolepsy, substance or medication contribution, or inability to function.

differential_and_comorbidity_notes: Consider circadian rhythm disorders, sleep apnea, narcolepsy, restless legs or periodic limb movement symptoms, thyroid disease, medication effects, pain, depression, anxiety, PTSD, and substance use.
Do not generate insomnia as a standalone psychiatric illness when symptoms are fully explained by another primary disorder unless the case context supports comorbidity.
special_populations: Older adults more often report sleep maintenance problems.
Pregnancy and menopause can contribute to sleep problems.
Caregivers and shift workers may have environmental or schedule-driven insomnia.
red_flags: Loud snoring, gasping, witnessed apneas, or high sleep apnea risk
Severe daytime sleepiness causing driving or occupational danger
New insomnia with mania-like decreased need for sleep, psychosis, suicidal ideation, or severe depression
Medication, alcohol, stimulant, or substance-related sleep disruption
Possible narcolepsy, circadian disorder, or neurologic symptoms requiring further evaluation
